title: 42nd Annual NABE Economic Policy Conference
summary: Goolsbee pushes back on the view that the economy is fragile and needs rapid FOMC easing, arguing 2025 growth was solid and broad-based (driven by the consumer, not AI investment) and that the labor market has been steady, with low net job creation explained by the immigration-driven drop in the break-even pace rather than weakening demand. With the real economy steady, he says the focus for near-term policy should return to inflation, which stalled around 3 percent core PCE and remains well above target, with services-ex-housing at a stubborn 3.3 percent. He stresses that at 3 percent inflation it is not obvious policy is even restrictive, and warns against front-loading too many cuts given repeated upside inflation surprises and tariff pass-through still in train. He remains open to more cuts this year but only if there is actual progress back toward 2 percent, leaving him patient and inflation-focused.

key_quotes.quote: With inflation at 3%, it is not obvious that our interest rate policy is even restrictive.
key_quotes.context: Judging the current stance as possibly already near neutral, implying little room to keep cutting

key_quotes.quote: I feel that front-loading too many rate cuts is not prudent in that circumstance.
key_quotes.context: Resisting rapid easing given inflation risks

key_quotes.quote: Three percent inflation is not good enough—and it’s not what we promised when the Federal Reserve committed to the 2% target.
key_quotes.context: Conviction that more progress on inflation is needed

key_quotes.quote: Economic growth and the labor market don’t seem especially fragile
key_quotes.context: Downplaying labor-market downside risk relative to inflation
